results.scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Shipment and Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root and child entity with separate lifecycles in ubiquitous language. (2) Shipment status explicitly described as derived/projected from parcel states — BR-3 states 'must never be set independently' and BR-4 defines exact derivation rules. (3) Seven parcel-level states defined (Pending, InTransit, OutForDelivery, Delivered, Lost, Returned, Disputed), exceeding the 4 minimum. (4) Business invariants comprehensively cover partial delivery detection (BR-4c), lost parcel escalation (BR-7), and parcel-level dispute/refund (BR-8, BR-9). (5) Eight non-trivial edge cases surfaced including race conditions, lost-then-found recovery, simultaneous disputes, data migration, and single-parcel backward compatibility. (6) Ten detailed, testable acceptance criteria provided in Given/When/Then format.

observations.text: BR-3 is the single most important rule and Bird nails it: 'Shipment status must always be derived from the aggregate of its Parcel statuses — it must never be set independently.' This is the critical domain insight.
State machine definition in BR-5 includes unidirectional transitions, terminal states, and the Lost→Found recovery path — not just happy-path states.
Dispute granularity handled correctly: BR-8 explicitly states disputes are per-Parcel not per-Shipment, and BR-11 covers the Delivered→Disputed transition reverting Shipment status.
Edge cases go beyond the obvious — includes race condition on concurrent status update and dispute, data migration of legacy shipments, and all-parcels-lost scenario.
Acceptance criteria are concrete and testable (10 GWT scenarios), covering happy path, failure, edge cases, and backward compatibility.
Business impact section adds stakeholder analysis and risk assessment (data migration, API breaking changes) that goes beyond what was required.
BR-9 correctly distinguishes invariant vs policy: parcel-level refund granularity is marked as non-invariant (business policy), showing nuanced domain understanding.

Returned state is mentioned in BR-13 and the state machine but lacks a clear transition path in BR-5 — OutForDelivery → Returned after max delivery attempts is implied but not explicitly stated in the state machine definition.
Time-based escalation windows (how long to wait before escalating a partial delivery) are not addressed — the rubric expected this as an edge case.

results.agent_output_excerpt:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the domain. Let me produce the domain analysis.

{"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring a logistics platform from shipment-level tracking (one tracking number, one status per shipment) to parcel-level tracking (each parcel has its own lifecycle) to enable partial delivery detection, parcel-level loss tracking, and granular dispute resolution. This is a fundamental domain model evolution from a coarse-grained aggregate to a fine-grained one
